title: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR THE PREVENTION AND REDUCTION OF ARSENIC CONTAMINATION IN RICE

sections.section: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR THE PREVENTION AND REDUCTION OF ARSENIC CONTAMINATION IN RICE
sections.summary: Document title only: Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Arsenic Contamination in Rice; no substantive provisions in this item.
Document identifier only (CXC 77-2017); no substantive regulatory content in this item.
Adoption metadata only: indicates the Code was adopted in 2017 and contains no substantive provisions.

Introduces arsenic as a toxic metalloid occurring in rice from natural and anthropogenic sources, explains rice absorbs arsenic especially under reducing (flooded) soil conditions and that arsenic occurs in inorganic and organic forms, and advises that effectiveness of control measures varies with local conditions so field studies across crop years are recommended to identify feasible measures and avoid unnecessarily restricting rice supply.

Scope: provides guidance to national/relevant food authorities, producers, manufacturers and others on preventing and reducing arsenic contamination in rice through source-directed measures and agricultural measures, and includes guidance on monitoring and risk communication.

Defines key terms including paddy, husked (brown) and polished (milled) rice, arsenic (inclusive of inorganic and organic forms), specifies inorganic arsenic (arsenite and arsenate) as the significant toxic form in rice, and defines flooded, aerobic and intermittent ponding cultivation conditions.

sections.section: 4. MEASURES TO PREVENT AND REDUCE ARSENIC CONTAMINATION
sections.summary: States inorganic arsenic is the most toxic form in rice and recommends prioritizing measures to reduce inorganic arsenic—particularly in highly contaminated areas—by implementing the source-directed measures in Section 4.3 and agricultural measures in Section 4.4 as appropriate.

Identifies natural and anthropogenic sources of arsenic (volcanic/geogenic sources, mining/smelting, fossil fuel burning, arsenic pesticides, CCA-treated timber, and contaminated agricultural inputs) and advises authorities to apply source-directed measures—referencing CXC 49-2001—including identifying/reducing/avoiding contaminated irrigation water and paddy fields and controlling industrial emissions, contaminated inputs and waste.

Recommends educating rice producers via technical guidance and farmer field schools and promoting practices (notably aerobic growth or intermittent ponding and selection of low-arsenic cultivars) to reduce arsenic uptake; it also warns aerobic/intermittent practices can increase cadmium or reduce yield and instructs risk managers to ensure cadmium does not reach unsafe levels and to consider source-directed cadmium controls where necessary.

Requires monitoring the effectiveness of arsenic-reduction measures by determining inorganic arsenic concentrations in rice and, where land or groundwater used for rice is widely contaminated, also monitoring arsenic in soil and/or irrigation water.

Advises authorities to communicate risks and benefits of polished versus husked rice and to share consumer and distributor guidance to reduce arsenic during processing and cooking (polishing to remove bran, washing or using rinse-free rice, and cooking with excess water and discarding it), and warns consumers not to use arsenic-contaminated water for washing or cooking rice.
